evals.prompt: I want to add retry logic, a cache layer, and a circuit breaker to this simple user fetch service. Is this a good idea?
evals.expected_output: No — these are features beyond the current task. Implement only what is asked for. Add them only if there is an explicit requirement.
assertions: Advises against adding the unrequested features
References not designing for hypothetical future requirements
Does not implement any of the three features

evals.prompt: Should I wrap template rendering in try/catch in case the view file is missing?
evals.expected_output: No — a missing view file is a deployment error, not a runtime condition. Let it throw. Catching it hides misconfiguration.
assertions: Advises against wrapping template rendering in try/catch
Explains that missing templates are deployment errors
Does not suggest adding a fallback or catch block

evals.prompt: My controller passes the entire $user object to every template. Is there a problem with that?
evals.expected_output: Yes — pass only the variables a template actually uses. Passing the full object floods templates with unneeded data. Extract only the required properties.
assertions: Identifies the anti-pattern of passing the full object
Recommends extracting only the properties the template uses
Does not rewrite code unless asked

evals.prompt: Should I add a comment above this foreach loop explaining what it does?
evals.expected_output: No — comments should only explain WHY, never WHAT. If the loop body uses well-named variables, the code is self-explanatory and needs no comment.
assertions: Advises against adding a comment that describes what the code does
Explains that comments should explain the why, not the what
